title: Collation of Key Textual Variants
description: This table collates significant textual variants across all witnesses of the Heart Sutra. Following Nattier (1992) and Attwood (2015, 2021), these variants help establish the stemmatic relationships between witnesses and identify later interpolations.

methodology.base_text: T251 (Xuanzang attribution, ca. 649-656 CE) as earliest datable witness
methodology.principle: Chinese compositional priority (Nattier 1992) - Sanskrit is back-translation from Chinese
methodology.critical_apparatus: Variants that distinguish original composition from later elaborations

variants.issue: Presence and form of opening salutation

T256.note: No maṅgala in Chinese; Sanskrit has simple form

variants.analysis: The simple 'namas sarvajñāya' in Hōryūji is original; the elaborate Tantric maṅgala in Nepalese MSS is a late interpolation reflecting Vajrayāna influence (Attwood 2021).
The Chinese requires accusative (object of 照見 'illuminated and saw'). Conze's nominative is a grammatical error. Attwood (2015) demonstrates accusative is correct.

variants.location: Section II: 'yad rūpaṃ sā śūnyatā' line
variants.issue: Presence of 'that which is form is emptiness' line

T256.note: Absent from Chinese; unclear in Sanskrit portion

Ja.text: yad rūpaṃ sā śūnyatā yā śūnyatā tad rūpam

Na-Ne.text: yad rūpaṃ sā śūnyatā yā śūnyatā tad rūpam

variants.analysis: This line is absent from all Chinese versions but present in Sanskrit. Nattier (1992) identified this as evidence that Sanskrit added material not in the Chinese original. The line may be a later expansion in Sanskrit tradition.

The na-aprāptiḥ is absent from T251, Fangshan, and crucially from the Hōryūji MS. Its presence in late Sanskrit MSS and Conze's edition is an interpolation. Huifeng (2014) argues original should be 'na prāptir nābhisamayaḥ' based on Large Sutra parallels.

The Chinese 究竟涅槃 and Hōryūji niṣṭhānirvāṇaḥ support reading as bahuvrīhi compound ('one whose nirvāṇa is complete'). Conze's addition of prāptaḥ follows late MSS and breaks the syntax. Attwood (2018a) provides detailed grammatical analysis.

The oṃ is absent from all early witnesses (T251, Fangshan, Hōryūji, T256, most Nepalese MSS). Only T257 (from late Tantric source) and late Nepalese MSS Nb and Ne include it. This is a Tantric interpolation based on the convention that all mantras begin with oṃ (Attwood 2021).
The Fangshan reading 薩婆訶 is actually more accurate as a transliteration of svāhā than T251's 僧莎訶, which appears corrupt. This suggests possible textual corruption in the received T251 tradition.

T250.text: 摩訶般若波羅蜜大明呪經

T257.text: 佛說聖佛母般若波羅蜜多經

variants.analysis: T250's title emphasizes mantra/dhāraṇī character. T251 and Sanskrit use 'heart' (心/hṛdaya). T257 calls Prajñāpāramitā 'Buddha-Mother'. Nepalese MSS show considerable title variation.

variants.issue: 觀自在 vs 觀世音
variants.analysis: 觀世音 (Guānshìyīn, 'Perceiver of Sounds') was the standard pre-Xuanzang translation. 觀自在 (Guānzìzài, 'Lord of Self-Perception') was Xuanzang's new translation introduced ca. 649 CE. This helps date T250 to before Xuanzang's influence.

variants.issue: Unique passage in T250 elaborating on skandha emptiness

T250.text: 色空故無惱壞相，受空故無受相，想空故無知相，行空故無作相，識空故無覺相。何以故？

variants.analysis: This passage, found only in T250, provides a detailed elaboration of how emptiness affects each skandha's characteristic. Its absence from T251 and all Sanskrit/Tibetan witnesses suggests T250 may preserve earlier material omitted in later traditions, or represents an independent parallel composition.
variants.scholarly_ref: First fully documented in this edition

variants.issue: Unique passage in T250 negating past, future, present

T250.text: 是空法，非過去，非未來，非現在。

variants.analysis: Temporal negation found in some Prajñāpāramitā sources. Its presence only in T250 supports the hypothesis that T250 either preserves earlier material or is an independent composition drawing on different Large Sūtra passages.
variants.scholarly_ref: First fully documented in this edition

variants.issue: T250 adds 一切 and 苦惱 to delusion passage

T251.note: 遠離 without 一切 or 苦惱

variants.analysis: T250's fuller form 離一切顛倒夢想苦惱 (separated from all perverted dream-thoughts and afflictions) differs from T251's 遠離顚倒夢想 (far removed from perverted dream-thoughts). This variant suggests different compositional choices rather than revision.
陰 (yīn) was the older translation for skandha, while 蘊 (yùn) was standardized by Xuanzang. T250's use of 陰 supports its pre-Xuanzang dating.

T250.text: 是大明呪、無上明呪、無等等明呪

T251.text: 是大神咒、是大明咒、是無上咒、是無等等咒

T257.text: 是大神咒、是大明咒、是無上咒、是無等等咒

variants.analysis: T250 has only three epithets, all using 明呪 (vidyāmantra). T251 and Sanskrit have four epithets. T250's divergence suggests an independent early tradition.

The Sanskrit uses kṣaya (destruction) matching Chinese 盡, not the standard Prajñāpāramitā term nirodha (cessation). This is key evidence for back-translation: the Sanskrit calqued from Chinese rather than quoting the Large Sutra directly (Nattier 1992).

The short recension (no frame) is original. The long recension with frame narrative was added later, probably to make the text conform to sūtra conventions. T257 was translated from the long recension tradition.

variants.issue: T250 consistently uses 波羅蜜 vs T251 波羅蜜多
variants.analysis: T250 consistently uses the shorter 波羅蜜 throughout, while T251 uses 波羅蜜多. The 多 (duō) represents the final syllable -tā of pāramitā. Earlier Chinese translations often omitted this; Xuanzang standardized inclusion. This consistent difference supports T250's earlier dating or independent composition.

variants.issue: T250 abbreviates bodhisattva; T251 gives full transliteration
variants.analysis: 菩薩 is an abbreviation of the full transliteration 菩提薩埵. Both were used in Chinese Buddhism, but 菩薩 became more common. T251's use of the full form in this formal context is characteristic of Xuanzang's translation style.

variants.issue: T250 uses 舍利弗 vs T251 舍利子
variants.analysis: Both are acceptable transliterations of Śāriputra. 舍利弗 (Shèlìfú) is phonetically closer; 舍利子 (Shèlìzǐ) uses 子 (zǐ, 'son') for -putra. The consistent difference throughout both texts suggests independent composition rather than revision.

stemma_notes.summary: Based on these variants, the witnesses can be organized into a provisional stemma.
early_chinese_tradition: T250 represents an independent early Chinese tradition (pre-Xuanzang)
T251/Fangshan represent the Xuanzang tradition (ca. 649-661 CE)
T256 documents Sanskrit as studied in Tang China
sanskrit_tradition: Hōryūji (Ja) is the earliest Sanskrit MS witness, close to the back-translation
Nepalese MSS show progressive Tantric elaboration (oṃ, maṅgala, frame narrative)
Conze's critical edition unfortunately privileged late Nepalese readings
late_translations: T257 was translated from elaborated Sanskrit long recension (1005 CE)
Its oṃ and na-aprāptiḥ come from its Sanskrit source, not Chinese tradition

scholarly_refs: Nattier, Jan (1992). 'The Heart Sūtra: a Chinese apocryphal text?' JIABS 15(2): 153-223.
Attwood, Jayarava (2015). 'Heart Murmurs: Some Problems with Conze's Prajñāpāramitāhṛdaya.' JOCBS 8: 28-48.
Attwood, Jayarava (2018a). 'A Note on Niṣṭhānirvāṇaḥ in the Heart Sutra.' JOCBS 14: 10-17.
Attwood, Jayarava (2021). 'Revised Editions of the Prajñāpāramitāhṛdaya and the Xīnjīng.' Draft.
Huifeng (2014). 'Apocryphal Treatment for Conze's Heart Problems.' JOCBS 6: 72-105.
Conze, Edward (1967). 'The Prajñāpāramitā-Hṛdaya Sūtra.' In Thirty Years of Buddhist Studies, pp. 147-167.
Silk, Jonathan (1994). 'The Heart Sūtra in Tibetan: A Critical Edition of the Two Recensions.' WSTB 34.
